coredata.dc:title: Ultrasound-assisted one-step reduction and self-assembly of carbon dots-reduced graphene oxide: Mechanism investigation and solid phase microextraction of ultra-trace organochlorine pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Shengrui
Liu, Hailin
Chen, Changpo
Feng, Suling
Fan, Jing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon dots-reduction graphene oxide (CDs/RGO) composite was synthesized by a facile and green strategy with one-step reduction and self-assembly of GO and CDs under ultrasonic treatment. CDs was successfully applied to reduce GO to RGO, and anchored on the surface of RGO simultaneously under ultrasonic treatment within 20 min. The reduction mechanism was demonstrated by density functional theory calculations, which confirmed the electron transfer from CDs to GO and supplemented the reduction mechanism of GO by CDs. Characterizations results showed that the as-prepared CDs/RGO composite exhibited lamellar spacing and wrinkled structures, and abundant chemical groups in comparison to RGO reduced by sodium borohydride. In view of excellent morphology and chemical structures, the CDs/RGO composite was used for the first time in extraction and determination of ultra-trace organochlorine pesticides (OCPs) from water by means of solid phase microextraction (SPME) coupled with gas chromatography-mass spectrometry. The CDs/RGO coating presented superior extraction capacities for OCPs compared with commercial SPME coatings and sodium borohydride reduced RGO. The proposed analytical method exhibited high sensitivity with detection limit of 0.003–0.051 ng/L, excellent linearity in concentration range of 0.050–2500 ng/L, and satisfactory precision for seven OCPs. The excellent performances made the proposed method to be applied in determination of ultra-trace OCPs in real water successfully. This work proposed a green approach to synthesize CDs/RGO composite and a potential coating sorbent for SPME.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs/RGO
Solid phase microextraction
Adsorption
Organochlorine pesticides

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of SPME fiber based on CDs/RGO composites
Collection and analysis of real water samples
Potential mechanisms for reduction of GO by CDs
Adsorption performance of CDs/RGO as SPME coating

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation for synthesis of CDs/RGO; (b) UV spectra; (c) XRD patterns; (d) Raman spectra for GO, CDs/RGO, and NaBH4-RGO.
SEM images of NaBH4-RGO (a) and CDs/RGO (b, c); TEM images of GO (d), CDs/RGO (e), and HRTEM images of CDs/RGO (f).
(a) XPS survey spectra of GO, CDs, CDs/RGO, and NaBH4-RGO; high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (b), O 1s (c) for CDs/RGO and NaBH4-RGO; (d) N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra for CDs/RGO.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (b) BJH pore size distribution for CDs/RGO and NaBH4-RGO; (c) TGA curve of CDs/RGO.

(a) Potential reaction pathways between GO and CDs; (b, c) charge density difference analysis of the CDs and GO with side view and top view, where the green color represents electron charge depletion, the yellow color corresponds to electron charge accumulation. The grey and red spheres represent C and O, respectively.

(a) Schematic procedures for SPME; (b) extraction efficiencies of CDs/RGO in comparison with NaBH4-RGO, PDMS (100 μm), and DVB/CAR/PDMS (75 μm) coatings; (c) reusability of CDs/RGO SPME coating. Error bars represent the standard deviations of the mean (n = 3).
Chromatograms of OCPs in campus tap water (A) and spiked tap water with OCPs concentration of 10 ng/L under SIM mode.
Analytical performance of as-prepared SPME coating towards OCPs.
Comparisons with other methods for SPME of OCPs from water.
Analytical results and recoveries for determination of OCPs from real water samples.

head.ce:title: Ultrasound-assisted one-step reduction and self-assembly of carbon dots-reduced graphene oxide: Mechanism investigation and solid phase microextraction of ultra-trace organochlorine pesticides

Xu).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions
Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon dots-reduction graphene oxide (CDs/RGO) composite was synthesized by a facile and green strategy with one-step reduction and self-assembly of GO and CDs under ultrasonic treatment. CDs was successfully applied to reduce GO to RGO, and anchored on the surface of RGO simultaneously under ultrasonic treatment within 20 min. The reduction mechanism was demonstrated by density functional theory calculations, which confirmed the electron transfer from CDs to GO and supplemented the reduction mechanism of GO by CDs. Characterizations results showed that the as-prepared CDs/RGO composite exhibited lamellar spacing and wrinkled structures, and abundant chemical groups in comparison to RGO reduced by sodium borohydride. In view of excellent morphology and chemical structures, the CDs/RGO composite was used for the first time in extraction and determination of ultra-trace organochlorine pesticides (OCPs) from water by means of solid phase microextraction (SPME) coupled with gas chromatography-mass spectrometry. The CDs/RGO coating presented superior extraction capacities for OCPs compared with commercial SPME coatings and sodium borohydride reduced RGO. The proposed analytical method exhibited high sensitivity with detection limit of 0.003–0.051 ng/L, excellent linearity in concentration range of 0.050–2500 ng/L, and satisfactory precision for seven OCPs. The excellent performances made the proposed method to be applied in determination of ultra-trace OCPs in real water successfully. This work proposed a green approach to synthesize CDs/RGO composite and a potential coating sorbent for SPME.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Since introduced by Pawliszyn and his co-workers in 1990 [1], solid-phase microextraction (SPME) attracted much attention as a powerful green technique for sample pretreatment owing to its outstanding advantages of high sensitivity, straightforward operation, and environmental friendliness. It has been widely used in environmental analysis, food analysis, medical analysis, biological analysis, and other fields [2–8].The extraction performance of SPME for target analyte mainly depends on the properties of coating materials. Although commercial SPME coatings such as PDMS, PA, DVB/PDMS, etc. were developed, more effort should be made to explore advanced materials for SPME coatings in order to analyze ultra-trace or specific compounds. Therefore, in the past few decades, researchers have devoted themselves to the development of advanced coatings, such as carbon nanomaterials [9,10], metal oxide composites [11,12], metal-organic frameworks [13,14], covalent organic frameworks [15,16], ionic liquid [17], and others.

Among the developed coating materials, graphene presented excellent adsorption and extraction performances for organic pollutants due to its large specific surface area, abundant π-electron structure, and hydrophobicity [18,19]. For instance, Zhang et al. [20] prepared a graphene coating on fused-silica fiber through layer-by-layer self-assembly strategy, and evaluated the SPME performances for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. Results showed that the as-prepared graphene coating displayed excellent adsorption capacities with analytical method detection limits of 1.52–2.72 ng/L. To further improve the adsorption capacity, graphene-based composites including polyaniline/graphene [21], graphene oxide/chitosan aerogel [22], polythiophene/grapheme [23], and others were developed subsequently. Wherein, the preparation of graphene and its composites played an important role on the adsorption performances. The chemical reduction of graphene oxide has been considered to be an efficient method to fabricate reduction grapheme oxide (RGO) owing to its outstanding advantages of simple operation, low cost, and easy to prepare in large scale [24]. However, severe agglomeration would be formed because of strong interaction among graphene oxide sheets, and numerous oxygen-containing groups vanished during chemical reduction process, which resulted in negative effect on adsorption capacity [25–28]. Furthermore, the use of toxic reducing regents such as hydrazine, hydroquinone, and sodium borohydride cannot meet the requirements of green chemistry. Therefore, a superior and environmental-friendly approach need to be further explored for preparing RGO from GO.

As a “zero-dimensional” quasi-spherical carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) has attracted much attention in the fields of sensing, drug delivery, and catalysis due to its unique photoelectric and fluorescence properties [29,30]. Besides, owing to its remarkable advantages of abundant oxygen-containing groups and π-electron structure, CDs also possesses excessive potential in adsorption capacity for organic compounds through hydrogen bonding, electrostatic adsorption, and π-π interactions [31]. However, it is difficult to separate and recycle CDs from aqueous solution after adsorption because of strong hydrophily, which limited its practical applications in capturing organic compounds from aqueous solution. To address this challenge, the functionalized support materials were used to immobilize the hydrophilic CDs. In our previous work, carbon dot decorated graphite carbon nitride composites (CDs/g-C3N4) were fabricated successfully and used as SPME coating material for efficient adsorption and detection of chlorobenzenes from water [32]. Results showed that the introduction of CDs improved the adsorption capacities distinctly for eight chlorobenzenes from water owing to its active groups. Moreover, it should be noticed that CDs has been proved to be a green reductant for the preparation of metal nano-particles [33,34]. Given the properties of reducibility and environmentally friendly, CDs presented the potential to prepare RGO by reduction of graphene oxide. Konwar et al. [35] reported a facile and green method for fabricating RGO from GO using CDs as reductant, and found that the as-prepared composites exhibited superior property in electrical conductivity.

In short, CDs have been proved to possess fine adsorption capacity for organic compounds, and exhibited great potential as the green reluctant. Therefore, preparation of CDs/RGO composites by one-step reduction and self-assembly of CDs and GO would take full advantage of their triple functions, which can be said to be three birds with one stone. Firstly, CDs was used as reductant for green preparation of RGO from GO. Secondly, CDs would be anchored onto RGO sheets during reduction process, which overcame the drawbacks of CDs as sorbent alone. Thirdly, abundant chemical groups on CDs were conducive to enhance the interaction for targeted compounds.

Herein, we developed a facile method to fabricate CDs/RGO composites by one-step reduction and self-assembly of CDs and GO under ultrasonic treatment. The preparation process and reduction mechanism were investigated by material characterizations and density-functional theory (DFT). CDs reduced and anchored onto GO sheet successfully, and enhanced the formation of wrinkled structure, lamellar spacing among RGO sheets, and abundant chemical groups, which played an important role in capturing targeted compounds. Furthermore, the as-prepared CDs/RGO composites were used as SPME coating for extraction and determination of ultra-trace organochlorine pesticides (OCPs) from water coupled with gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS). With results that CDs/RGO coating presented excellent adsorption capacity, and the proposed analytical method possessed superior sensitivity for OCPs in comparison to other methods reported in literatures. Notably, OCPs including chloroneb, trifluralin, hexachlorobenzene, chlorpyrifos, g-chlordance, a-chlordane, chlorobenzilate in water have become the key monitoring pollutants due to their wide application, high toxicity, and low degradability [36–39]. Therefore, determination of OCPs from water was used to evaluate the performances of as-prepared CDs/RGO composites.

The chemical regents, materials, and instruments used in this work for preparation of CDs/RGO composites, fabrication of SPME fiber, and characterizations were detailed in supplementary information (Section S1).

Synthesis of CDs and GO. The preparation of CDs was carried out using hydrothermal method according to literatures with modifications [40]. Briefly, 5.25 g citric acid and 1.7 mL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water firstly. Then, the solution was transferred to an autoclave, and held at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooled to room temperature, the obtained brown-black solution was filtered with a 0.45 μm filter membrane, and dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis membrane with molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da. Finally, CDs powder was obtained after drying at 120 °C in an oven. The synthesis of GO solution was performed using modified Hummers method. The details of GO preparation procedures were shown in Section S2.

Synthesis of CDs/RGO composites. The CDs/RGO composites were synthesized by a facile and wild method with ultrasonic treatment. Briefly, 5 mL GO (6.9 mg mL−1) solution and 5 mL CDs solution with concentration of 4 mg mL−1 were mixed to be a uniform suspension. Then, the mixture was placed into a probe sonicator (JY92-IIN, Ningbo Scientz Biotechnology Co., Ltd, Ningbo, China) with frequency of 20 kHz for ultrasonic treatment of 20 min. After that, the generated precipitate was collected by centrifugation, and washed thoroughly with distilled water and ethanol for three times, respectively. Finally, the CDs/RGO composite was obtained after dried in vacuum oven at 70 °C. Moreover, RGO that reduced by sodium borohydride (NaBH4-RGO) was also fabricated in order to compare with CDs/RGO composites. The synthesis procedures were detailed in Section S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of SPME fiber based on CDs/RGO composites
ce:section.ce:para: The SPME fiber based on CDs/RGO nanocomposite material was fabricated using stainless-steel wire as substrate and silicone sealant as adhesion agent according to previous reported methods [41,42]. Typically, the stainless-steel wire was cut into 3–4 cm in length and washed with hydrochloric acid and ethanol, respectively. The cleaned stainless-steel wire was immersed in diluted silicone sealant solution and pulled out to form a uniform silicone sealant coating on the surface of stainless-steel wire. Then, the stainless-steel wire was immersed into CDs/RGO composites powder to form the coating. To obtain sufficient coating thickness, the coating steps were repeated two times. After dried at 120 °C for 30 min, the as-prepared fiber was equipped onto an empty SPME needle by bending the uncoated end of stainless steel wire to a certain angel with tweezers to ensure the fiber being locked in the inner tube. Before extraction procedure, the SPME fiber was aged in GC–MS injector at 250 °C for 20 min.

All the extractions were carried out in 20 mL vials containing 10 mL samples. Typically, the SPME fiber was immersed into solution, and held for a certain time. After extraction, the SPME fiber was injected into the GC–MS injector for desorption and analysis. Wherein, a heater was used to heat the sample, and a magnetic rod with a rotational speed of 300 rpm was put into vial to accelerate the extraction. The extraction temperature and time were set at 30–70 °C and 10–50 min for conditions optimization. The detection of OCPs was performed by GC–MS operated with SIM mode for monitoring the characteristic ions. The operation parameters, total ion chromatogram, and characteristic ions of seven OCPs were detailed in Section S4, Fig. S1, and Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection and analysis of real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real river samples were collected from Wei River near campus, Wei River near agricultural land, and campus tap water, numbered as 1#, 2#, and 3#, respectively. The collected water samples were filtered with 0.45 μm membranes, and then were transported to the laboratory for extraction and analysis by the proposed method.

Fig. 1 a displays the facile fabrication procedure of CDs/RGO composites by ultrasonic treatment. It can be seen that the GO and CDs solution presented brown colors, after ultrasonic treatment with 20 min, the CDs/RGO composites was generated in forms of dark flocculus. To confirm the roles of CDs on the reduction of GO, the GO solution was treated by ultrasonication, with results that no flocculent precipitate was formed (Fig. S2). Moreover, it can be observed from Fig. S3 that visible flocculus was generated for mixture of CDs and GO after 36 h at room temperature without other treatment. The results demonstrated that CDs played the key role on reduction of GO, and ultrasonic treatment facilitated the reaction rate distinctly.

To confirm the formation of CDs/RGO composites, UV spectrum was used to characterize the prepared materials. As shown in Fig. 1b, the presence of wide peak at ∼296.9 nm for GO was ascribed to n-π* transition of CO bond [35,43]. After ultrasonic treatment with 20 min, the adsorption peak of GO vanished and emerged a new peak at 284.7 nm, which indicated that GO was reduced to be RGO [44]. XRD was used to characterized the crystal structures of GO, CDs/RGO and NaBH4-RGO (Fig. 1c). It can be seen that GO presents a sharp diffraction peak at 9.8°, and after ultrasonic treatment with CDs, the diffraction peak at 25.6° appears, which corresponds to RGO. Raman spectra were also recorded to further demonstrate the reduction reaction of GO by CDs. As shown in Fig. 1d, two peaks are observed at 1342 cm−1 and 1589 cm−1, corresponding to D-band and G-band respectively, wherein, the D band represents the defect of the carbon lattice, and the G band represents the in-plane stretching vibration of the sp2 hybrid of the carbon atom [45]. The intensity ratios of D band and G band (ID/IG) increase gradually from 1.0056 (GO) to 1.025 (CDs/RGO). The increase of ID/IG can be ascribed to the increase of defects caused by the removal of oxygen-containing functional groups during the reduction process [43]. These results further confirmed the reduction of GO.

SEM and TEM characterizations were performed to obtain the morphologies of CDs/RGO. Fig. 2 a shows the SEM image of RGO prepared by NaBH4 as reducing agent. The as-prepared NaBH4-RGO displays an irregular bulk structure composed of RGO layers. It was found that severe agglomeration formed for NaBH4-RGO due to the interaction of π-π stacking among RGO layers. In contrast, a loosened composite with structures of abundant lamellar spacing and wrinkled morphologies was observed using CDs as reductant under ultrasonic treatment (Fig. 2b and c). The formation of lamellar spacing and wrinkled structures were beneficial to produce higher surface area, thus resulting in superior adsorption capacity for targeted compounds. TEM image in Fig. 2d displays a typical sheet structure for as-prepared GO. It can be obviously seen from Fig. 2e that CDs are decorated on the RGO sheet simultaneously during reduction process. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2f) further confirmed the presence of CDs on the RGO sheet by distinct lattice fringes with interplanar distances of 0.27 nm that corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of sp2 graphite carbon [46].

To demonstrate the chemical compositions of as-prepared materials, XPS analysis of CDs, GO, CDs/RGO, and NaBH4-RGO were investigated in this work. As shown in Fig. 3 a, XPS survey spectra of all the materials exhibit two strong peaks at 285.5 and 532.7 eV, which are attributed to C 1s and O 1s. Besides, a peak at 400.2 eV corresponded to N 1s was detected in survey spectra of CDs and CDs/RGO due to the existence of nitrogen. It is worth noting that the oxygen content of NaBH4-RGO decreases from 31.84 to 28.13 % relative to GO due to the reduction effect. However, the oxygen content of CDs/RGO increases to 32.07 % (Table S2). This result can be explained that the introduction of CDs improved the oxygen-containing groups, which was beneficial to interact with targeted compounds. To further explore the chemical groups on the materials surface, the XPS peaks of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s were deconvoluted into fine peaks. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s for CDs/RGO, and NaBH4-RGO (Fig. 3b) are divided into four peaks at 284.5, 285.2, 286.7, and 287.5 eV, which are assigned to CC, CC, CO, and CO, respectively [47,48]. Compared with NaBH4-RGO, the as-prepared CDs/RGO composites exhibit more abundant CO and CO groups owing to the introduction of CDs. The high resolution spectra of O 1s (Fig. 3c) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 532.3 and 531.5 eV, which corresponded to C–OH/COC and CO, respectively [25,43]. The N 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into two peaks with binding energies at 399.9 and 401.0 eV referring to CN and NH, respectively. The formation of abundant oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups has been proved to possess great potentials in capturing compounds with polar groups [49–51].

The BET surface area and pore size of CDs/RGO and NaBH4-RGO were investigated by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and by the BJH method. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the specific surface area of CDs/RGO composites was calculated to be 231.6 m2 g−1, which was much higher than that of NaBH4-RGO (15.6 m2 g−1). The pore size distribution of CDs/RGO composites (Fig. 4b) displays two distinct regions ranging from 1.8 to 4.5 nm and 5 to 48 nm with an average pore size of 24 nm. However, there is no obvious pore emerge for NaBH4-RGO. The generation of plentiful pores for CDs/RGO composites was ascribed to the formation of wrinkled surface and lamellar spacing among RGO sheets. Large surface area and wide pore size distribution increased the adsorption sites and enhanced the molecule diffusion into inner surface, thus resulting in higher adsorption capacity for targeted compounds. Furthermore, the thermal stability of as-prepared materials is also a crucial factor for SPME. Fig. 4c displays the TG curve of CDs/RGO at temperature range of 25–800 °C under nitrogen condition. It can be found that the CDs/RGO composites present excellent thermal stability within 350 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential mechanisms for reduction of GO by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the characterization results of as-prepared materials confirmed the reduction of GO to RGO using CDs as reductant with ultrasonic treatment. The potential mechanisms can be described as the Fig. 5 a according to literatures with modifications [35,52,53]. Typically, the hydroxyl groups of CDs would react with the oxygen-containing groups on the GO, leading to deoxidization following with elimination of water molecules. Herein, two possible reaction paths were investigated. One path was performed by the reaction of hydroxyl on CDs and epoxy groups on GO with generation of ether groups, and then producing a double carbon bond and a water molecule (Fig. 5a (i)). The other path was taken place by the reaction of hydroxyl groups on GO and CDs, resulting in the formation of olefins for RGO and elimination of water molecule, and the hydroxyl groups on CDs were oxidized to carbonyl groups (Fig. 5a (ii)). The reaction paths revealed that the RGO structures were restored to be sp2 hybridised aromatic ring structure during the reduction process.

To further demonstrate the reduction mechanism, the electron transfer between CDs and GO was investigated by density-functional theory (DFT) including charge density difference and Bader charge analysis. The details of specific calculation process are explained in Section S5, where a two-dimensional flake structure with crystal constants of a = 21.37, b = 17.28 and c = 15.00 was selected as the model for simulated calculation. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the electrons accumulated on the interface of CDs and GO, and transferred into GO from CDs. Meanwhile, a certain electron depletion district emerged on the CDs that far away from interface. Moreover, the electron transfer quantity was also explored by Bader charge analysis, with results that the average transferred charge was calculated to be 0.1904 e. The DFT calculation results confirmed the electron transfer from CDs to GO, and supplemented the reduction mechanism of GO by CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption performance of CDs/RGO as SPME coating
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption and capture performances of as-prepared CDs/RGO composites was evaluated through extracting OCPs from spiked water with concentration of 5 ng/L by means of SPME coupled with GC–MS (Fig. 6 a). The fabricated SPME probe presented a uniform coating on the surface of stainless steel wire with coating thickness of ∼45 µm. To observe the optimum extraction performance for OCPs, the effects of extraction conditions including extraction temperature, extraction time, ion strength, and solution pH on extraction efficiency of CDs/RGO SPME coating were investigated. The discussion details of conditions optimization were shown in Section S6 and Fig. S4. As shown in Fig. 6b, the as-prepared CDs/RGO SPME coating presented superior extraction efficiencies for seven OCPs in comparison to commercial SPME coatings such as 100 μm PDMS, 75 μm DVB/CAR/PDMS and NaBH4-RGO under the optimum extraction conditions, owing to its abundant chemical groups, unique structure, and large surface area. Moreover, we evaluated the lifetime of as-prepared CDs/RGO coating by repeat extraction of OCPs from water. It was found that no distinct elimination in extraction efficiency occurred after 120 reuses (Fig. 6c). The results indicated that the CDs/RGO coating exhibited excellent reusability.

To reveal the adsorption mechanism of as-prepared CDs/RGO composites towards OCPs, adsorption kinetics and adsorption isotherms were explored using chloroneb, hexachlorobenze, g-chlordane, and a-chlordane as the selected compounds. The details of experiments, calculation methods, and results for adsorption kinetics and adsorption isotherms are shown in Section S7 and Section S8. Adsorption kinetics fitting results showed that the adsorption rate of OCPs corresponded to pseudo-second-order kinetic model (Fig. S5, Table S3), which confirmed the existence of chemisorption. Moreover, the Weber’s intraparticle diffusion model was also used to demonstrate the adsorption process, with results that the adsorption rate was controlled by both the boundary layer effect and the intra-particle diffusion (Fig. S6). For the adsorption isotherms, Langmuir model can better demonstrate the adsorption process than Freundlich model (Fig. S7, Table S4), indicating that the adsorption process depended on the monolayer adsorption. The experimental maximum adsorption capacities for targeted compounds were consistent with the calculated results.

To sum up, the adsorption mechanism of CDs/RGO for OCPs was attributed to the following three factors according to the characterization results, adsorption kinetics and adsorption isotherms explorations. Firstly, the hydrophobic interactions and π-π stacking between targeted compounds and composites surface played a dominant role for OCPs adsorption. Secondly, abundant oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups on the composites were beneficial to interact with compounds those involving polar groups, which have been confirmed by XPS analysis. Thirdly, the formation of wrinkled surface and lamellar spacing among RGO sheets facilitated compounds diffusion from outer surface into inner surface, and enhanced the adsorption active sites for OCPs effectively.

The proposed method was used to analyze OCPs from three collected water samples. As shown in Table 3 , hexachlorobenzene was detected with concentration of 0.13 ng/L in river water sample collected near agricultural land due to the possible usage of pesticides. The recoveries for all the samples with spiked concentration of 0.5, 5, and 10 ng/L were in the range of 73.3–115.5 %. The chromatograms of OCPs in campus tap water and spiked tap water (10 ng/L) under SIM mode are shown in Fig. 7 , with results that all the targeted OCPs compounds was detected in spiked tap water. Satisfactory recoveries confirmed the practicability of proposed analytical method in determination of real water samples.

In conclusion, a facile and green method was proposed to fabricated CDs/RGO composite by one-step reduction and self-assembly of GO and CDs with ultrasonic treatment. It was found that the prepared CDs/RGO composite presented lamellar spacing and wrinkled structures, and abundant chemical groups, which enhanced the mass transfer and adsorption capacity for targeted compounds. Moreover, the electron transfer from CDs to GO during reduction process was confirmed by DFT calculations. Finally, the CDs/RGO composite was used to capture ultra-trace OCPs from water by means of SPME coating. Results showed that the CDs/RGO coating exhibited superior adsorption performance, and the proposed method presented high sensitivity, excellent linearity, and good precision for seven OCPs. Moreover, the CDs/RGO coating also presented satisfactory extraction capacities towards OCPs from fruit juice (Section S9 and Fig. S8). This work provided a green method for fabrication of CDs/RGO composite, confirmed the reduction capacity of CDs, and proposed a potential sorbent for SPME coating.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21976052, 21977025, 21705017), the Youth Science Foundation of Henan Normal University (20200183). We also would like to thank Zhenyun Huang from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the DFT calculations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.138569.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solid phase microextraction with thermal desorption using fused silica optical fibers
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.L. Arthur, J. Pawliszyn, Solid phase microextraction with thermal desorption using fused silica optical fibers, Analy. Chem. 62(19) (1990) 2145-2148.

Trends Anal. Chem.

Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Gionfriddo, E.A. Souza-Silva, T.D. Ho, J.L. Anderson, J. Pawliszyn, Exploiting the tunable selectivity features of polymeric ionic liquid-based SPME sorbents in food analysis, Talanta 188 (2018) 522-530.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemotyping the medicinal herb Egletes viscosa through SPME-GC-MS
